sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2. Blue at rows 36-39, cols 20-39. Magenta at rows 56-59, cols 40-43. Two red blocks as obstacles. N timer at bottom.
- Goal model: Complete level 2. Based on level 1, the winning pattern is: move blue to upper position, align right edge with magenta's right edge, press SPACE.
- Plan: Try UP, RIGHT (to align blue right edge at col 43 with magenta right edge), SPACE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 3 of about 58 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blue moved from rows 36-39, cols 20-39 to rows 32-35, cols 24-43 after UP, RIGHT, SPACE. So:
- UP moved the blue from rows 36-39 to rows 32-35
- RIGHT moved the blue from cols 20-39 to cols 24-43
- SPACE didn't cause a jump (the blue stayed at rows 32-35, cols 24-43)
Wait, but in level 1, SPACE caused the blue to jump from rows 12-15 to rows 36-39. Here, SPACE didn't cause a jump. Why?
Let me think about what was different:
- In level 1, the blue was at rows 12-15 (upper position) before SPACE
- In level 2, the blue is at rows 32-35 after UP
Maybe the blue needs to be at a specific row position for SPACE to trigger the jump. In level 1, the blue had two positions: rows 12-15 (upper) and rows 16-19 (lower). SPACE was pressed when the blue was at the upper position (rows 12-15).
In level 2, the blue started at rows 36-39. After UP, it's at rows 32-35. Maybe there are more positions in level 2. Let me check if the blue can move further UP.
Actually, wait. In level 1, the blue's two positions were rows 12-15 and rows 16-19. The difference was 4 rows. In level 2, the blue started at rows 36-39 and after UP moved to rows 32-35. The difference is also 4 rows.
But in level 1, the blue could only be at two positions. In level 2, maybe the blue can be at more positions? Or maybe the blue needs to be at a specific position for SPACE to work.
Let me try pressing SPACE again at the current position (rows 32-35, cols 24-43). Or let me try moving the blue to different positions and pressing SPACE.
Actually, let me reconsider the level 1 solution. In level 1:
- Blue started at rows 16-19, cols 12-31
- UP moved to rows 12-15
- RIGHT×3 moved to cols 24-43
- SPACE caused the jump to rows 36-39, cols 20-39
The key might be that the blue was at the UPPER position (rows 12-15) and its right edge was at col 43 (which is the magenta's right edge + 4... no, magenta was at cols 36-39, so right edge is 39).
Wait, in level 1, the magenta was at cols 36-39, and the blue's right edge after RIGHT×3 was at col 43. But the blue ended up at cols 20-39, which has right edge at col 39 (magenta's right edge).
Hmm, let me reconsider. In level 1:
- Before SPACE: blue at rows 12-15, cols 24-43
- After SPACE: blue at rows 36-39, cols 20-39
- The blue's row range changed from 12-15 to 36-39 (the magenta's column range!)
- The blue's column range changed from 24-43 to 20-39 (right edge at magenta's right edge)
So the rule seems to be:
- After SPACE, the blue's row range = magenta's column range
- After SPACE, the blue's right edge = magenta's right edge
In level 1:
- Magenta: rows 4-7, cols 36-39
- Blue after SPACE: rows 36-39, cols 20-39
- Row range: 36-39 = magenta's col range ✓
- Right edge: 39 = magenta's right edge ✓
In level 2:
- Magenta: rows 56-59, cols 40-43
- Expected blue after SPACE: rows 40-43, cols 24-43
- Row range: 40-43 = magenta's col range
- Right edge: 43 = magenta's right edge
But when I pressed SPACE at rows 32-35, cols 24-43, the blue didn't jump. Maybe the blue needs to be at a specific starting position for SPACE to trigger the jump.
In level 1, the blue was at rows 12-15 (the upper position) before SPACE. Maybe in level 2, the blue needs to be at a specific upper position too.
Let me try moving the blue further UP and then pressing SPACE. Currently the blue is at rows 32-35. Let me try UP again to see if it moves further.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Level 2. Blue at rows 32-35, cols 24-43 after UP, RIGHT, SPACE. SPACE didn't cause a jump this time. In level 1, the blue was at the upper position (rows 12-15) when SPACE triggered the jump. Maybe I need to move the blue further UP.
Goal model: Complete level 2. Need to find the right position for SPACE to trigger the jump.
Plan: Try moving UP more to see if the blue can reach a higher position, then press SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue is at rows 32-35, cols 24-43
# Try moving UP more to see if there are more positions
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
b_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
if b_nodes:
    print(f"Blue after UP: rows {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][0]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][1]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][1][1]}")
print(f"board_changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
b_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
if b_nodes:
    print(f"Blue after UP×2: rows {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][0]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][1]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][1][1]}")

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
b_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
if b_nodes:
    print(f"Blue after UP×3: rows {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][0]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {b_nodes[0]['boundary'][0][1]}-{b_nodes[0]['boundary'][1][1]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
